perturbation.original_text: 11. Term. This Agreement shall become effective upon execution by the parties and shall continue in full force and effect for a period of two (2) years from the date thereof, provided however, that the obligations of the parties contained herein shall continue for a period of five (5) years following the date of such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Term. This Agreement shall become effective upon execution by the parties and shall continue in full force and effect for a period of two (2) years from the date thereof, provided however, that the obligations of the parties contained herein shall continue for a period of one (1) year following the date of such termination.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality obligation period to one year after termination may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Ohio law, which recognizes a potentially longer period depending on the nature of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61 defines trade secret misappropriation and implies that the duration of trade secret protection should be based on how long the information remains confidential and provides a competitive advantage, not necessarily a fixed one-year period. A one-year post-termination obligation may be deemed insufficient if the trade secret retains its value for a longer duration.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.61 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.61 | Uniform trade secrets act definitions. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: July 20, 1994 Latest Legislation: House Bill 320 - 120th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF As used in sections 1333.61 to 1333.69 of the Revised Code, unless the context requires otherwise: (A) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (B) "Misappropriation" means any of the following: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without the express or implied consent of the other person by a person who did any of the following: (a) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (b) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret that the person acquired was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (c) Before a material change of their position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (C) "Person" has the same meaning as in division (C) of section 1.59 of the Revised Code and includes governmental entities. (D) "Trade
perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the post-termination obligation period for trade secret protection from two years to one year. The Law Explanation states that Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61 implies that the duration of trade secret protection should be based on how long the information remains confidential and provides a competitive advantage, not necessarily a fixed one-year period. The Perturbation Explanation states that this reduction may be insufficient under Ohio law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' and 'Improper Means' related to trade secrets, indicating the importance of protecting trade secrets. Given that the legally recognized duration of trade secret protection is tied to its continued competitive advantage, a fixed one-year term may be insufficient and thus contradicts the implied requirement of the law for a longer protection period if the trade secret's value persists beyond one year.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Return of Documents. Upon written request by Client at any time, Chemsultants shall promptly return all Confidential Information, including all copies, reproductions, summaries, analyses or extracts thereof or based thereon in the party’s p o s s e s s i o n o r i n t h e p o s s e s s i o n o f a n y R e p r e s e n t a t i v e o f t h e p a r t y , p r o v i d e d , however, Chemsultants shall be permitted to charge Client for all reasonable costs incurred by Chemsultants for compliance with this Section.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Return of Documents. Upon written request by Client at any time, Chemsultants shall return all Confidential Information, including all copies, reproductions, summaries, analyses or extracts thereof or based thereon in the party’s possession or in the possession of any Representative of the party, provided, however, Chemsultants shall be permitted to charge Client for all costs incurred by Chemsultants for compliance with this Section, including unreasonable costs.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing Chemsultants to charge the client for unreasonable costs related to the return of confidential information contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in Ohio contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: ORC § 1301.304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract or duty within the UCC, which by analogy, extends to general contract law in Ohio. Allowing for the charging of "unreasonable costs" violates this implied covenant, as it allows Chemsultants to profit unfairly from its obligations under the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1301.304 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1301.304 | Obligation of good faith - UCC 1-304. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1301 General Provisions Effective: June 29, 2011 Latest Legislation: House Bill 9 - 129th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF Obligation of good faith [UCC 1-304] Every contract or duty within Chapters 1301., 1302., 1303., 1304., 1305., 1307., 1308., 1309., and 1310. of the Revised Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Available Versions of this Section June 29, 2011 – House Bill 9 - 129th General Assembly [ View June 29, 2011 Version ]

perturbation.justification: The modified text allows Chemsultants to charge the client for "unreasonable costs" related to returning confidential information. This directly contradicts the obligation of good faith required in the performance of every contract as stated in ORC § 1301.304, presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1: "Every contract or duty within Chapters 1301., 1302., 1303., 1304., 1305., 1307., 1308., 1309., and 1310. of the Revised Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." Charging unreasonable costs undermines the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, creating a legal contradiction. The altered text enables unfair profit from contractual obligations, going against the principle of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Disclosures Required by Law. In the event Chemsultants is requested by applicable law or regulation or by legal process to disclose any Confidential Information or any other information concerning Client, Chemsultants agrees that it will provide Client with prompt notice of such request or requirement in order to enable Client to seek an appropriate protective order or other remedy, to consult with Client with respect to taking steps to resist or narrow the scope of such request or legal process, or to waive compliance, in whole or in part, with the terms of this Agreement. In any such event Chemsultants will use its reasonable best efforts to ensure that all Confidential Information and other information that is so disclosed will be accorded confidential treatment.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Disclosures Required by Law. In the event Chemsultants is requested by applicable law or regulation or by legal process to disclose any Confidential Information or any other information concerning Client, Chemsultants agrees that it will provide Client with notice of such request or requirement within 30 days in order to enable Client to seek an appropriate protective order or other remedy, to consult with Client with respect to taking steps to resist or narrow the scope of such request or legal process, or to waive compliance, in whole or in part, with the terms of this Agreement. In any such event Chemsultants will use its reasonable best efforts to ensure that all Confidential Information and other information that is so disclosed will be accorded confidential treatment.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification to 30 days may impede the client's ability to promptly seek a protective order, contradicting the duty to cooperate during discovery under Ohio Civil Rule 26(C).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ohio Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 26(C)
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Civil Rule 26(C) governs protective orders in discovery. Delaying notice for 30 days before disclosing confidential information prevents the Client from getting a prompt hearing to protect trade secrets and confidential information in court as intended under Rule 26(C).

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'prompt notice' for disclosing confidential information, enabling the client to seek protective measures. The modified text changes this to 'within 30 days,' creating a delay. The Law Explanation indicates that this delay prevents the Client from getting a prompt hearing to protect trade secrets and confidential information in court as intended under Rule 26(C). The Scraped Law Snippet 1, while partially unreadable, pertains to Ohio Civil Rule 26(C), which governs protective orders in discovery, reinforcing that the delay undermines the client's ability to promptly seek protective orders.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Miscellaneous. This Agreement shall be governed by, construed, and enforced in accordance with the laws, and in the courts, of the State of Ohio, County of Lake, without giving effect to any conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Miscellaneous. This Agreement shall be governed by, construed, and enforced in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction selected by Chemsultants, and in the courts, of the State of Ohio, County of Lake, without giving effect to any conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing Chemsultants to unilaterally select the governing law after the contract is signed undermines the principle of mutual agreement and potentially violates Ohio public policy regarding contracts of adhesion.
perturbation.law_explanation: While ORC § 1345.03 primarily addresses unfair or deceptive consumer sales practices, Ohio courts often apply similar principles to commercial contracts where there is a significant imbalance of power. Allowing one party to unilaterally choose the governing law post-agreement raises concerns about fairness and potential overreach, conflicting with the underlying principles of balanced contractual obligations that Ohio law seeks to uphold.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1345.03 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1345.03 | Unconscionable consumer sales acts or practices. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1345 Consumer Sales Practices Effective: April 6, 2017 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 227 - 131st General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) No supplier shall commit an unconscionable act or practice in connection with a consumer transaction. Such an unconscionable act or practice by a supplier violates this section whether it occurs before, during, or after the transaction. (B) In determining whether an act or practice is unconscionable, the following circumstances shall be taken into consideration: (1) Whether the supplier has knowingly taken advantage of the inability of the consumer reasonably to protect the consumer's interests because of the consumer's physical or mental infirmities, ignorance, illiteracy, or inability to understand the language of an agreement; (2) Whether the supplier knew at the time the consumer transaction was entered into that the price was substantially in excess of the price at which similar property or services were readily obtainable in similar consumer transactions by like consumers; (3) Whether the supplier knew at the time the consumer transaction was entered into of the inability of the consumer to receive a substantial benefit from the subject of the consumer transaction; (4) Whether the supplier knew at the time the consumer transaction was entered into that there was no reasonable probability of payment of the obligation in full by the consumer; (5) Whether the supplier required the consumer to enter into a consumer transaction on terms the supplier knew were substantially one-side
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Section 1345.03 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1345.03 | Unconscionable consumer sales acts or practices. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1345 Consumer Sales Practices Effective: April 6, 2017 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 227 - 131st General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) No supplier shall commit an unconscionable act or practice in connection with a consumer transaction. Such an unconscionable act or practice by a supplier violates this section whether it occurs before, during, or after the transaction. (B) In determining whether an act or practice is unconscionable, the following circumstances shall be taken into consideration: (1) Whether the supplier has knowingly taken advantage of the inability of the consumer reasonably to protect the consumer's interests because of the consumer's physical or mental infirmities, ignorance, illiteracy, or inability to understand the language of an agreement; (2) Whether the supplier knew at the time the consumer transaction was entered into that the price was substantially in excess of the price at which similar property or services were readily obtainable in similar consumer transactions by like consumers; (3) Whether the supplier knew at the time the consumer transaction was entered into of the inability of the consumer to receive a substantial benefit from the subject of the consumer transaction; (4) Whether the supplier knew at the time the consumer transaction was entered into that there was no reasonable probability of payment of the obligation in full by the consumer; (5) Whether the supplier required the consumer to enter into a consumer transaction on terms the supplier knew were substantially one-side
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces an inconsistency that undermines the principle of mutual agreement, potentially conflicting with Ohio law. The original contract specifies Ohio law as the governing law, whereas the changed contract allows Chemsultants to unilaterally select the governing law. This alteration could lead to the application of laws less favorable to the other party. While the provided snippets from ORC § 1345.03 primarily concern consumer sales practices and 'unconscionable act or practice', the Law Explanation suggests Ohio courts apply similar principles to commercial contracts where there's a significant power imbalance. The altered text potentially creates an unconscionable situation by allowing one party to unilaterally change the rules after the agreement is made.
